title: Registers & volumes: a bound book and its entries
audience: Civil registration, parish & institutional registers, minute books, ships' logs
intro: A register is one bound book full of many entries — baptisms, minutes, log days. The entries aren't separate records floating free; they're components of the volume. This scenario branches on single-volume vs a run of volumes, models the entries as Record Parts, and attaches the creating body.
question: Is a register volume a Record, and each entry a Record Part? Who is the creator?

steps.prompt: One volume, or a run of volumes?

choices.label: A single volume
choices.why: Good — one bound volume is one Record; its entries are components of it.

choices.label: A run of volumes
choices.why: Good — a continuous run (e.g. minute books 1900–1950) is a Record Set, each volume a member Record.

choices.label: A Record Part
choices.why: Not the volume. An entry within the volume is a Record Part; the volume itself is a Record.

choices.label: Record
choices.why: Fits. The bound volume is a Record; its individual entries are Record Parts of it; the physical book is an Instantiation.

choices.label: Record Set
choices.why: Not for one book. Use a Record Set only for a run of volumes. A single volume with internal entries is a Record with Record Parts.

choices.label: Instantiation
choices.why: That's the physical book as an object. Its intellectual content is the Record.

capture.comment: Create the volume as a Record.

choices.label: Record Set
choices.why: Fits. The run is an aggregation of volumes — a Record Set — each volume a member Record (which in turn has its entries as Record Parts).

choices.label: Record
choices.why: Undersells it — a run is many volumes spanning years. Use a Record Set of volume Records.

choices.label: Record Part
choices.why: Doesn't fit. The run is an aggregation, not a component.

capture.comment: Create the run as a Record Set.

steps.prompt: A specific entry needs its own description (its date, the people named). How do you model it?

choices.label: Record Part
choices.why: Fits. An entry is a component with independent content that completes the volume — a Record Part, with its own date and the people it names as relations. Describe only the entries you need to; the rest stay implicit in the volume.

choices.label: A separate Record
choices.why: Usually too much. An entry isn't an independent record — it only makes sense inside its volume. Use a Record Part (unless an entry is genuinely a standalone document).

choices.label: A row in a spreadsheet attached to the volume
choices.why: Doesn't fit the model. To be linked to people, dates and places, the entry needs to be an entity — a Record Part.

capture.comment: Create the entry as a Record Part of the volume.

steps.prompt: Who created the register — and how do you record it?

choices.label: Corporate body (Agent) + has_creator
choices.why: Fits. The creating body — the parish, the council, the registrar's office — is a Corporate Body, which is a kind of Agent. Relate the register to it with has_creator; the same Agent is the creator of all its registers.

choices.label: Type the parish name in the title
choices.why: Doesn't fit. The creating body should be a reconcilable Agent so every register it kept is findable together.

capture.comment: Create the creating body as an Agent (corporate body).
Link the register to its creating body with has_creator.

outcome.verdict: Volume, entries and creator — one connected book.
outcome.summary: The volume is a Record (or the run a Record Set); the entries you care about are Record Parts with their own dates and named people; and the creating body is a reusable Corporate-Body Agent. The register stops being an opaque book and becomes searchable down to the entry.

links.label: Record Part (RiC-E05) in the model navigator
